title: Build a Tiny Transformer
description: Implement a complete transformer model from scratch. Build a nanoGPT-style architecture, understand the full forward pass from tokenization through training to text generation. This brings together all previous concepts into a working language model.

sections.title: Architecture Overview
sections.content:
## Complete Transformer: From Tokens to Text We now assemble all components into a complete transformer capable of training on text data and generating coherent samples. ### Model Architecture Overview A minimal but functional transformer (GPT-style decoder-only) consists of: 1. **Token Embedding**: Token ID → dense vector of size `embed_dim`. 2. **Positional Embedding**: Add position information (learned or sinusoidal). 3. **Stack of N Transformer Blocks**, each: - LayerNorm - Multi-head self-attention (with causal mask) - Residual add - LayerNorm - Feed-forward MLP (`d → 4d → d`) - Residual add 4. **Final LayerNorm + Output Linear** (`embed_dim → vocab_size`). 5. **Softmax** over vocabulary for next-token probabilities. The shape `(B, T, d)` is preserved through every block. Only the final output projection changes the last dimension to `vocab_size`. ```mermaid flowchart TB IN(["token_ids (B, T)"]) --> TE["Token Embedding
(V, d)"] IN --> PE["Position Embedding
(T_max, d)"] TE --> ADD0((+)) PE --> ADD0 ADD0 --> X0(["x (B, T, d)"]) X0 --> BLK["N × Transformer Block
shape preserved"] BLK --> LNF["Final LayerNorm"] LNF --> OUT["Output Linear (d → V)"] OUT --> LOG(["logits (B, T, V)"]) classDef tile fill:#161b22,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#58a6ff classDef blk fill:#161b22,stroke:#bc8cff,color:#bc8cff classDef green fill:#161b22,stroke:#3fb950,color:#3fb950 class IN,X0,LOG tile class BLK blk class OUT,LNF green ``` Notation: `B` = batch size, `T` = sequence length, `d` = embedding dim, `V` = vocab size.

sections.title: Pre-Norm & Residuals
sections.content:
## Pre-Norm vs Post-Norm Modern transformers (GPT-2, Llama, Mistral) use **pre-norm**: LayerNorm is applied *before* the sublayer, and the residual is added *after*. The original 2017 Transformer used **post-norm**: the residual is added first, then LayerNorm is applied to the sum. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Pre["Pre-Norm (modern: GPT-2, Llama)"] PA(["x"]) --> PB["LayerNorm"] PB --> PC["Sublayer
(Attn or MLP)"] PC --> PD((+)) PA --> PD PD --> PE(["y"]) end subgraph Post["Post-Norm (original Transformer 2017)"] XA(["x"]) --> XB["Sublayer
(Attn or MLP)"] XB --> XC((+)) XA --> XC XC --> XD["LayerNorm"] XD --> XE(["y"]) end ``` Pre-norm is preferred because: - The residual path is **identity-clean**: gradients flow from output back to input through pure additions, never through a normalization. - Training is stable for deep networks (24+ layers) without warmup tricks. - Reaches better final loss in practice. ### Single Transformer Block (Pre-Norm) Each block is two sublayers (attention then MLP), each wrapped in pre-norm + residual: ```mermaid flowchart TB X(["x (B, T, d)"]) --> LN1["LayerNorm"] LN1 --> ATT["Multi-Head
Self-Attention
(causal mask)"] ATT --> ADD1((+)) X --> ADD1 ADD1 --> LN2["LayerNorm"] LN2 --> MLP["FFN
Linear(d → 4d) → GELU/ReLU → Linear(4d → d)"] MLP --> ADD2((+)) ADD1 --> ADD2 ADD2 --> Y(["y (B, T, d)"]) classDef res fill:#161b22,stroke:#3fb950,color:#3fb950 class ADD1,ADD2 res ``` ### Residual Connections: The Key to Deep Networks Residual connections compute `y = x + f(x)`. They are essential because: 1. **Gradient highways**: gradients reach early layers without being squashed by nonlinearities. 2. **Identity default**: if `f` is initialized small, the layer starts as identity and *learns* what to add. 3. **Depth**: networks with 12, 32, 96+ layers train reliably; without residuals, networks past ~20 layers struggle (this is the historical motivation for ResNet). Residuals are why we can stack many transformer blocks and still train.

sections.title: Feed-Forward & Forward Pass
sections.content:
## Feed-Forward Network

The MLP between attention layers is surprisingly important — it holds most of the model's parameters and provides per-token nonlinear computation:

```
MLP(x) = activation(x W_1 + b_1) W_2 + b_2
```

with `W_1 ∈ ℝ^(d × 4d)` and `W_2 ∈ ℝ^(4d × d)`. The hidden width is typically `4 × d`.

**Why factor 4?**
- Empirically robust across model scales.
- Provides enough capacity for the activation (ReLU, GELU, or SwiGLU) to act as a per-token "key-value memory".
- Modern large models (Llama, Mistral) often use SwiGLU, which is *narrower* (~`8/3 × d`) because the gating doubles the effective parameter count. Small models stay at `4 × d`.

## Full Forward Pass (Shapes)

With `B = 2`, `T = 10`, `V = 10000`, `d = 512`:

1. **Tokenize**: text → token IDs of shape `(B, T)`.
2. **Embed**: `(B, T)` → `(B, T, d)` via `Embedding(V, d)`.
3. **Add positions**: `(B, T, d) + (T, d)` → `(B, T, d)` (broadcast over batch).
4. **For each block** (`× N`):
   - `LayerNorm`: `(B, T, d)` → `(B, T, d)`
   - `Attention(causal)`: `(B, T, d)` → `(B, T, d)`
   - `Add residual`: `(B, T, d)`
   - `LayerNorm`: `(B, T, d)` → `(B, T, d)`
   - `MLP`: `(B, T, d)` → `(B, T, d)`
   - `Add residual`: `(B, T, d)`
5. **Final LayerNorm + Linear head**: `(B, T, d)` → `(B, T, V)` logits.
6. **Loss**: cross-entropy over flattened `(B·T, V)` logits vs `(B·T,)` targets.

Shapes are preserved end-to-end except the final projection. This invariance is what lets you stack as many blocks as your compute budget allows.

sections.title: Training vs Inference
sections.content:
## Training vs Inference The same model is run in two very different modes: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TR["Training (parallel)"] TI["full sequence (B, T)"] --> TM["Transformer
(causal mask)"] TM --> TL["logits (B, T, V)
cross-entropy on all positions"] end subgraph GE["Inference (sequential)"] GS["seed tokens"] --> GL["forward last token
(B, 1) + KV cache"] GL --> GP["sample next token"] GP -- "append, repeat" --> GS end ``` **Training** processes a full `(B, T)` block in parallel. Causal masking ensures position `t` cannot peek at positions `> t`, so a single forward pass yields `T` separate next-token-prediction losses. Cross-entropy is averaged over all positions. **Inference** generates one token at a time: feed the seed, sample the next token from `logits[:, -1, :]`, append, repeat. A KV cache (Module 5) avoids recomputing keys/values for prior positions. ### Decoding Strategies Getting from `logits` to a token is its own subfield: - **Argmax (greedy)**: `argmax(logits[:, -1])`. Deterministic, often repetitive. - **Temperature sampling**: divide logits by `T` before softmax. `T<1` sharpens (more deterministic), `T>1` flattens (more diverse). `T=1` is the model's true distribution. - **Top-k sampling**: keep only the `k` highest logits, sample from those. - **Top-p (nucleus) sampling**: keep the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability ≥ `p`. Adapts to the model's confidence. - **Beam search**: deterministic. Maintain `B` partial hypotheses, expand each by every vocab token, keep the `B` best by cumulative log-prob. Preferred for translation, summarization, and structured output where you want the *most likely* sequence rather than a sample. Implemented in HuggingFace via `model.generate(..., num_beams=4)`. For open-ended chat / story generation, top-p sampling at `p=0.9` and `T≈0.8` is a common default. For tasks with a single correct answer, beam search or greedy decoding is usually better. ### EOS and Stop Tokens A real generator does not loop a fixed `max_new_tokens` — it halts when the model emits an end-of-sequence token (e.g. `<|endoftext|>` for GPT-2, `` for Llama). The standard pattern: ```python for _ in range(max_new_tokens): logits, _ = self(generated[:, -context_length:]) next_token = sample(logits[:, -1]) generated = torch.cat([generated, next_token], dim=1) if next_token.item() == eos_token_id: break ``` Note the distinction: `max_new_tokens` caps tokens *generated* (excluding the prompt), while `max_length` caps total length (prompt + generation). HuggingFace's `generate()` accepts both; mixing them up is a common source of off-by-prompt-length bugs. ## Scaling Laws Kaplan et al. (2020) found that test loss scales as a power law in model size `N` (parameter count): ``` L(N) ≈ (N_c / N)^α_N with α_N ≈ 0.076 ``` A 10× increase in parameters lowers loss by a factor of `10^(-0.076) ≈ 0.84`, i.e. roughly a **16% reduction** in cross-entropy per decade of params (with data and compute scaled appropriately). Chinchilla (Hoffmann et al., 2022) refined this: for a fixed compute budget, model size and training tokens should scale roughly equally — earlier models were undertrained. Scaling laws let you plan: "to halve loss, I need roughly `10^(0.5/0.076) ≈ 10^6.6×` parameters" — a sobering number that explains why frontier models cost what they do. ## Modern Variations The core blueprint (attention + residuals + LayerNorm + MLP) is remarkably stable. Common modern tweaks: - **RoPE (Rotary Position Embeddings)**: rotate Q and K by position-dependent angles instead of adding learned position embeddings. - **ALiBi**: replace position embeddings with a linear bias on attention scores. - **RMSNorm**: drop the mean-centering from LayerNorm; faster, equally effective. - **SwiGLU**: gated MLP `(silu(x W_g) ⊙ x W_u) W_d`; supplants ReLU/GELU in Llama-family models. - **GQA / MQA**: share key/value heads across query heads to shrink the KV cache (Module 5). - **No dropout**: large modern models (Llama, Mistral) drop regularization entirely — at scale, the data itself regularizes. - **Weight tying**: share weights between input embedding and output projection (`output_head.weight = token_embedding.weight`). Saves `V·d` parameters. Knowing which knobs exist helps you read modern model code without surprise.

sections.title: Putting It All Together: NanoGPT
sections.content:
## Build a Working NanoGPT

The code below is a complete, trainable nanoGPT-style model. A few things to notice:

### Causal Mask: Boolean, Not Float 0/1

`nn.MultiheadAttention` interprets its `attn_mask` argument **based on dtype**:

- **Float** (`float32`, etc.) → treated as an **additive bias** to attention scores. `-inf` masks a position; `0.0` keeps it.
- **Bool** → `True` means "mask this position out"; `False` means "keep".

A float `0/1` lower-triangular tensor (i.e. `torch.tril(torch.ones(T, T))`) is *silently wrong*: PyTorch adds `1.0` to scores at allowed positions and `0.0` at disallowed ones, which is just a small bias — **causality is not enforced**, and the model can attend to future tokens during training. Generation will still produce text, but the model is leaking targets into its inputs.

The correct boolean form is:

```python
# True where we want to MASK OUT (positions strictly above the diagonal)
causal_mask = torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, dtype=torch.bool), diagonal=1)
```

Or the additive form:

```python
# 0.0 on/below diagonal, -inf above diagonal
causal_mask = torch.zeros(T, T)
causal_mask.masked_fill_(torch.triu(torch.ones(T, T, dtype=torch.bool), diagonal=1), float('-inf'))
```

The code below uses the boolean form.

### Other Implementation Notes

- `LayerNorm` is computed **once** per residual branch, not three times for Q/K/V separately.
- `register_buffer` keeps the causal mask on the same device as the model without making it a learnable parameter.
- Weight initialization is left to PyTorch defaults; production models use scaled-init (e.g. `std=0.02`).

## Common Pitfalls

1. **Float 0/1 causal mask** passed to `nn.MultiheadAttention.attn_mask` — silent correctness bug; causality is *not* enforced (see above).
2. **Forgetting `model.eval()` during generation** — keeps dropout active, making samples noisier than they should be.
3. **Wrong loss reduction with padding** — must mask padding tokens (`F.cross_entropy(..., ignore_index=pad_id)`) or the model learns to predict padding.
4. **No gradient clipping** — transformers can spike to NaN under unlucky batches; `clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)` is standard.
5. **Aggressive learning rate without warmup** — transformers prefer linear warmup over a few hundred steps before cosine decay.
6. **Temperature too high during generation** — incoherent. Too low — repetitive loops. Start at `T=0.8`, top-p `0.9`.
7. **Validating on training-overlap text** — char-level LMs memorize fast; keep a held-out split.
8. **Loop forever in `generate()`** — always check for EOS and bound by `max_new_tokens`.
sections.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math


def build_causal_mask(seq_len: int, device=None) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Boolean causal mask for nn.MultiheadAttention.
    Shape (seq_len, seq_len). True means MASK OUT (i.e. position cannot attend there).
    The upper triangle (strictly above the diagonal) is True.
    """
    return torch.triu(
        torch.ones(seq_len, seq_len, dtype=torch.bool, device=device),
        diagonal=1,
    )


class TransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    """Single transformer block: attention + MLP with pre-norm and residuals"""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim, num_heads=8, mlp_ratio=4, dropout=0.1):
        super().__init__()

        # Attention block
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)
        self.attention = nn.MultiheadAttention(
            embed_dim, num_heads, dropout=dropout, batch_first=True
        )

        # MLP block
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)
        mlp_hidden = int(embed_dim * mlp_ratio)
        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(embed_dim, mlp_hidden),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Dropout(dropout),
            nn.Linear(mlp_hidden, embed_dim),
            nn.Dropout(dropout),
        )

        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, x, attn_mask=None):
        """
        Args:
            x: (batch, seq_len, embed_dim)
            attn_mask: (seq_len, seq_len) BOOLEAN causal mask (True = mask out)
                      or float (-inf/0) additive mask. NEVER a 0/1 float mask.
        """
        # Pre-norm: compute LayerNorm ONCE, reuse for Q, K, V.
        xn = self.norm1(x)
        attn_output, _ = self.attention(xn, xn, xn, attn_mask=attn_mask, need_weights=False)
        x = x + self.dropout(attn_output)

        # Pre-norm MLP
        mlp_output = self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        x = x + self.dropout(mlp_output)
        return x


class NanoGPT(nn.Module):
    """Minimal but complete transformer language model (GPT-style)."""
    def __init__(
        self,
        vocab_size,
        embed_dim=384,
        num_layers=6,
        num_heads=8,
        context_length=256,
        dropout=0.1,
    ):
        super().__init__()

        self.vocab_size = vocab_size
        self.embed_dim = embed_dim
        self.context_length = context_length

        # Embeddings
        self.token_embedding = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, embed_dim)
        self.position_embedding = nn.Embedding(context_length, embed_dim)
        self.embedding_dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)

        # Transformer blocks
        self.transformer_blocks = nn.ModuleList([
            TransformerBlock(embed_dim, num_heads, mlp_ratio=4, dropout=dropout)
            for _ in range(num_layers)
        ])

        # Output
        self.final_norm = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)
        self.output_head = nn.Linear(embed_dim, vocab_size)

        # Optional: weight tying (share embedding and output head weights).
        # self.output_head.weight = self.token_embedding.weight

        # Register BOOLEAN causal mask as a buffer so it moves with .to(device).
        # True = mask out. Shape (context_length, context_length).
        self.register_buffer(
            "causal_mask",
            torch.triu(
                torch.ones(context_length, context_length, dtype=torch.bool),
                diagonal=1,
            ),
            persistent=False,
        )

    def forward(self, token_ids, targets=None):
        """
        Args:
            token_ids: (batch, seq_len) with token indices
            targets:   (batch, seq_len) target token indices for loss, or None

        Returns:
            logits: (batch, seq_len, vocab_size)
            loss:   scalar if targets provided, None otherwise
        """
        batch_size, seq_len = token_ids.shape
        assert seq_len <= self.context_length, \
            f"sequence length {seq_len} exceeds context_length {self.context_length}"

        # Embeddings
        token_emb = self.token_embedding(token_ids)             # (B, T, d)
        positions = torch.arange(seq_len, device=token_ids.device, dtype=torch.long)
        pos_emb = self.position_embedding(positions)            # (T, d) -> broadcasts over B
        x = self.embedding_dropout(token_emb + pos_emb)         # (B, T, d)

        # Slice the causal mask to current seq_len. BOOLEAN, True=mask out.
        # nn.MultiheadAttention accepts a 2D mask of shape (T, T).
        attn_mask = self.causal_mask[:seq_len, :seq_len]

        for block in self.transformer_blocks:
            x = block(x, attn_mask=attn_mask)

        x = self.final_norm(x)
        logits = self.output_head(x)                            # (B, T, V)

        loss = None
        if targets is not None:
            loss = F.cross_entropy(
                logits.view(-1, self.vocab_size),
                targets.view(-1),
            )
        return logits, loss

    @torch.no_grad()
    def generate(self, seed_tokens, max_new_tokens=100, temperature=1.0,
                 top_k=None, eos_token_id=None):
        """
        Autoregressive generation with optional EOS halting.

        Args:
            seed_tokens: (B, T0) initial token IDs
            max_new_tokens: cap on generated tokens (excluding the prompt)
            temperature: softmax temperature; lower = more deterministic
            top_k: if set, sample only from top-k logits
            eos_token_id: if set, stop when ALL sequences in the batch emit it

        Returns:
            generated: (B, T0 + n) for some n <= max_new_tokens
        """
        self.eval()
        generated = seed_tokens.clone()
        finished = torch.zeros(generated.size(0), dtype=torch.bool, device=generated.device)

        for _ in range(max_new_tokens):
            # Crop context to model's window
            context = generated[:, -self.context_length:]
            logits, _ = self(context)
            next_logits = logits[:, -1, :] / max(temperature, 1e-8)

            if top_k is not None:
                top_logits, top_idx = torch.topk(next_logits, top_k, dim=-1)
                filtered = torch.full_like(next_logits, float('-inf'))
                filtered.scatter_(1, top_idx, top_logits)
                next_logits = filtered

            probs = F.softmax(next_logits, dim=-1)
            next_token = torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=1)   # (B, 1)

            # Once a sequence is finished, force it to keep emitting EOS
            # so that the batch stays aligned in shape.
            if eos_token_id is not None:
                next_token[finished] = eos_token_id
                finished = finished | (next_token.squeeze(-1) == eos_token_id)

            generated = torch.cat([generated, next_token], dim=1)

            if eos_token_id is not None and finished.all():
                break

        return generated


# ===== Sanity check =====

if __name__ == "__main__":
    vocab_size = 256
    context_length = 64
    model = NanoGPT(
        vocab_size=vocab_size,
        embed_dim=128,
        num_layers=4,
        num_heads=4,
        context_length=context_length,
    )

    # Confirm the registered mask is BOOLEAN and upper-triangular (strict)
    assert model.causal_mask.dtype == torch.bool
    assert model.causal_mask[0, 1].item() is True   # position 0 cannot attend to position 1
    assert model.causal_mask[1, 0].item() is False  # position 1 CAN attend to position 0

    batch_size, seq_len = 4, 32
    token_ids = torch.randint(0, vocab_size, (batch_size, seq_len))
    targets   = torch.randint(0, vocab_size, (batch_size, seq_len))

    logits, loss = model(token_ids, targets)
    print(f"Logits: {tuple(logits.shape)}, Loss: {loss.item():.4f}")

    seed = torch.zeros((1, 1), dtype=torch.long)
    out = model.generate(seed, max_new_tokens=50, temperature=0.8, top_k=40)
    print(f"Generated: {tuple(out.shape)}")

    loss.backward()
    print(f"Params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters()):,}")

references.title: Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al., 2017)
Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners (GPT-2)
Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models (Kaplan et al., 2020)
Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models (Chinchilla, Hoffmann et al., 2022)

quiz.question: What is the purpose of pre-norm in modern transformer architectures?
options: To reduce parameter count
To normalize input before passing to attention/MLP, enabling better gradient flow through residual connections
To increase training speed
To prevent overfitting
quiz.explanation: Pre-norm (LayerNorm before the sublayer, then add residual) allows gradients to flow directly through skip connections without passing through normalization. This stabilizes training of deeper networks compared to post-norm.

quiz.question: Why are residual connections (y = x + f(x)) essential in transformers?
options: They reduce the number of parameters
They provide gradient highways and allow very deep networks to train effectively
They speed up computation
They improve the final accuracy
quiz.explanation: Residual connections enable gradients to bypass nonlinearities, reaching early layers directly. Without them, deep networks (>50 layers) struggle to train. With residuals, we can train 100+ layer networks effectively.

quiz.question: In the feed-forward network within each block, what is the purpose of the expansion from d to 4d?
options: To reduce memory usage
To add non-linearity and expressiveness through a wider hidden layer
To match the attention computation
To compute attention heads
quiz.explanation: The MLP expands from embedding dimension d to 4d (or another factor), applies a non-linearity (ReLU/GELU/SwiGLU), then projects back to d. This bottleneck adds expressiveness. Factor 4 is empirical; SwiGLU-based modern models often use ~8/3·d because gating doubles effective parameters.

quiz.question: What is the main difference between training and inference for autoregressive generation?
options: Training uses all examples, inference uses only one
Training processes full sequences at once with causal masking; inference generates one token at a time using KV cache
Inference is faster because it uses simpler computations
Training requires more memory
quiz.explanation: During training, we process full sequences in parallel and compute loss on all positions (causal mask prevents looking ahead). During inference, we generate one token per step (feeding it back as input), using a KV cache to avoid recomputing keys and values.

quiz.question: How does temperature affect text generation?
options: Temperature does not affect generation quality
Higher temperature = more random/diverse; Lower temperature = more deterministic/repetitive
Higher temperature = more deterministic; Lower temperature = more random
Temperature only affects decoding speed
quiz.explanation: Temperature scales the logits before softmax. T=1 is standard. T>1 flattens logits (more uniform distribution, more random). T<1 sharpens logits (higher prob for top choices, more deterministic, can become repetitive).

quiz.question: In the original Transformer, where is dropout applied?
options: Only at the model input and output
After each sublayer's residual connection (and on attention weights), but disabled during inference
Only on the FFN's expansion layer
Inside each LayerNorm
quiz.explanation: Vaswani et al. apply dropout to the output of each sublayer before adding the residual, plus on the embedding sum and on attention weights (after softmax). Dropout is disabled at inference via model.eval(). Modern large LMs (Llama, Mistral) often drop regularization entirely because data scale itself regularizes.

exercise.title: Train a Tiny Transformer on TinyShakespeare
exercise.description: Implement a complete NanoGPT model and train it on the TinyShakespeare dataset (~1MB of Shakespeare plays — the same corpus Karpathy uses). You must: (1) build the full transformer architecture (with a CORRECT boolean causal mask), (2) create a character-level tokenizer, (3) implement a training loop with gradient clipping, (4) track train/val loss, (5) generate text samples during training that should start showing words like 'Lord', 'thou', 'King' after a few minutes on GPU. Best run in Colab via the link above. Target loss on TinyShakespeare with this small model: ~1.5 nats/char val loss after 10 epochs is decent; world-class char-LMs reach ~1.0.
exercise.starterCode:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader
import urllib.request
import os

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 0: Download TinyShakespeare (~1MB). Karpathy's canonical dataset.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TINYSHAKESPEARE_URL = (
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/char-rnn/master/"
    "data/tinyshakespeare/input.txt"
)

def load_tinyshakespeare(path="input.txt"):
    if not os.path.exists(path):
        urllib.request.urlretrieve(TINYSHAKESPEARE_URL, path)
    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        return f.read()


class CharacterDataset(Dataset):
    """Character-level dataset for language modeling."""
    def __init__(self, text, context_length=64):
        # TODO: build sorted list of unique characters as the vocabulary
        # TODO: create char_to_idx and idx_to_char mappings
        # TODO: encode the entire text into a 1D LongTensor of token IDs
        # TODO: store context_length
        pass

    def __len__(self):
        # TODO: number of (context, target) pairs we can extract
        pass

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        # TODO: return (context, target) where target is context shifted by 1
        pass

    def decode(self, token_ids):
        # TODO: convert a 1D iterable of token IDs back to text
        pass


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 1: Build the model.
#
# IMPORTANT: nn.MultiheadAttention's attn_mask must be BOOLEAN (True = mask out)
# or FLOAT additive (-inf / 0). A float 0/1 lower-triangular mask is
# treated as an additive bias and does NOT enforce causality.
# Use torch.triu(..., diagonal=1).bool() for the boolean form.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

class TransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, embed_dim, num_heads=4, dropout=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        # TODO: norm1, attention (nn.MultiheadAttention with batch_first=True)

exercise.solution:
import os
import urllib.request
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader

TINYSHAKESPEARE_URL = (
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/char-rnn/master/"
    "data/tinyshakespeare/input.txt"
)

def load_tinyshakespeare(path="input.txt"):
    if not os.path.exists(path):
        urllib.request.urlretrieve(TINYSHAKESPEARE_URL, path)
    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        return f.read()


class CharacterDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, text, context_length=64):
        self.context_length = context_length
        self.chars = sorted(set(text))
        self.vocab_size = len(self.chars)
        self.char_to_idx = {c: i for i, c in enumerate(self.chars)}
        self.idx_to_char = {i: c for c, i in self.char_to_idx.items()}
        self.data = torch.tensor(
            [self.char_to_idx[c] for c in text], dtype=torch.long
        )

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.data) - self.context_length - 1

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        ctx = self.data[idx : idx + self.context_length]
        tgt = self.data[idx + 1 : idx + self.context_length + 1]
        return ctx, tgt

    def decode(self, token_ids):
        return ''.join(self.idx_to_char[int(t)] for t in token_ids)


class TransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, embed_dim, num_heads=4, dropout=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)
        self.attention = nn.MultiheadAttention(
            embed_dim, num_heads, dropout=dropout, batch_first=True
        )
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)
        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(embed_dim, 4 * embed_dim),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Dropout(dropout),
            nn.Linear(4 * embed_dim, embed_dim),
            nn.Dropout(dropout),
        )

    def forward(self, x, attn_mask=None):
        # IMPORTANT: attn_mask must be BOOLEAN (True = mask out) or FLOAT additive.
        xn = self.norm1(x)
        attn, _ = self.attention(xn, xn, xn, attn_mask=attn_mask, need_weights=False)
        x = x + attn
        x = x + self.mlp(self.norm2(x))
        return x


class NanoGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab_size, embed_dim=128, num_layers=3,
                 num_heads=4, context_length=64, dropout=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.vocab_size = vocab_size
        self.embed_dim = embed_dim
        self.context_length = context_length

        self.token_embedding = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, embed_dim)
        self.position_embedding = nn.Embedding(context_length, embed_dim)

        self.blocks = nn.ModuleList([
            TransformerBlock(embed_dim, num_heads, dropout)
            for _ in range(num_layers)
        ])

        self.final_norm = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)
        self.output_head = nn.Linear(embed_dim, vocab_size)

        # BOOLEAN causal mask, True = mask out (strictly above diagonal).
        self.register_buffer(
            "causal_mask",
            torch.triu(
                torch.ones(context_length, context_length, dtype=torch.bool),
                diagonal=1,
            ),
            persistent=False,
        )

    def forward(self, token_ids, targets=None):
        B, T = token_ids.shape
        token_emb = self.token_embedding(token_ids)
        positions = torch.arange(T, device=token_ids.device)
        x = token_emb + self.position_embedding(positions)

        attn_mask = self.causal_mask[:T, :T]   # BOOL, True=mask out

        for block in self.blocks:
            x = block(x, attn_mask=attn_mask)

        logits = self.output_head(self.final_norm(x))

        loss = None
        if targets is not None:
            loss = F.cross_entropy(
                logits.view(-1, self.vocab_size), targets.view(-1)
            )
        return logits, loss

    @torch.no_grad()
    def generate(self, seed, max_new_tokens=200, temperature=1.0,
                 top_k=None, eos_token_id=None):
        self.eval()
        out = seed.clone()
        for _ in range(max_new_tokens):
            ctx = out[:, -self.context_length:]
            logits, _ = self(ctx)
            next_logits = logits[:, -1, :] / max(temperature, 1e-8)
            if top_k is not None:
                v, idx = torch.topk(next_logits, top_k, dim=-1)
                filt = torch.full_like(next_logits, float('-inf'))
                filt.scatter_(1, idx, v)
                next_logits = filt
            probs = F.softmax(next_logits, dim=-1)
            next_tok = torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=1)
            out = torch.cat([out, next_tok], dim=1)
            if eos_token_id is not None and (next_tok == eos_token_id).all():
                break
        return out


def train():
    device = (
        "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available()
        else "mps" if torch.backends.mps.is_available()
        else "cpu"
    )
    print(f"Device: {device}")

    text = load_tinyshakespeare()
    dataset = CharacterDataset(text, context_length=64)
    print(f"Vocab size: {dataset.vocab_size}, samples: {len(dataset):,}")

    n_train = int(0.9 * len(dataset))
    n_val = len(dataset) - n_train
    train_ds, val_ds = torch.utils.data.random_split(
        dataset, [n_train, n_val],
        generator=torch.Generator().manual_seed(42),
    )
    train_loader = DataLoader(train_ds, batch_size=64, shuffle=True, num_workers=0)
    val_loader   = DataLoader(val_ds,   batch_size=64, shuffle=False, num_workers=0)

    model = NanoGPT(
        vocab_size=dataset.vocab_size,
        embed_dim=128,
        num_layers=3,
        num_heads=4,
        context_length=64,
    ).to(device)
    print(f"Params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters()):,}")

    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=3e-4)

    for epoch in range(10):
        model.train()
        train_loss = 0.0
        for x, y in train_loader:
            x, y = x.to(device), y.to(device)
            logits, loss = model(x, y)
            optimizer.zero_grad()
            loss.backward()
            torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
            optimizer.step()
            train_loss += loss.item()
        train_loss /= len(train_loader)

        model.eval()
        val_loss = 0.0
        with torch.no_grad():
            for x, y in val_loader:
                x, y = x.to(device), y.to(device)
                _, loss = model(x, y)
                val_loss += loss.item()
        val_loss /= len(val_loader)

        # Train/val gap: large gap -> overfitting; both flat -> underfitting.
        # On TinyShakespeare with this size, expect val ~1.5 nats/char by epoch 10.
        print(f"Epoch {epoch+1:2d} | train {train_loss:.4f} | val {val_loss:.4f}")

        if (epoch + 1) % 2 == 0:
            seed_text = "ROMEO:"
            seed_ids = torch.tensor(
                [[dataset.char_to_idx[c] for c in seed_text]],
                dtype=torch.long, device=device,
            )
            sample = model.generate(seed_ids, max_new_tokens=200, temperature=0.8, top_k=40)
            print("--- sample ---")
            print(dataset.decode(sample[0].tolist()))
            print("--------------")

    return model


if __name__ == "__main__":
    train()
